MathModel's blog

Codeforces Round 1061 (Div. 2) Editorial
By MathModel, history, 5 months ago,

Thank you for participating !

Special Thanks to hxu10 for giving me the chance to write the editorial and for maomao90 for enhancing the editorial !.

Editorial of Problems E and F are written by hxu10, and the rest are written by me. I hope they're insightful and concise, please let me hear your opinion about it or any feedback in general 💙.

2156A - Pizza Time

Hints
Hint 1

Since m1≤m2m1≤m2, the total number of slices Alice ate is always at least as much as the total number of slices Bob ate. What's optimal construction ?

Hint 2

We want to try to minimize the difference between the total number of slices Alice ate and the total number of slices Bob ate.

Hint 3

The optimal construction is to let both m1m1 and m2m2 to be equals to one. The sequence of operations, denoted by (m1,m2,m3)(m1,m2,m3), is as follows:

(1,1,n−2)→(1,1,n−4)→…(1,1,n−2)→(1,1,n−4)→…
Solution

The general form of the sequence

(1,1,n−2)→(1,1,n−4)→…(1,1,n−2)→(1,1,n−4)→…

Note that we're considering the operations when m1≤m2≤m3m1≤m2≤m3.

Consider the case where nn is even:

(1,1,n−2)→(1,1,n−4)→…→(1,1,2)(1,1,n−2)→(1,1,n−4)→…→(1,1,2)

The number of operations is the length of the sequence 2,4,6,…,2(n2−1)2,4,6,…,2(n2−1) which is n2−1n2−1.

Consider the case where nn is odd:

(1,1,n−2)→(1,1,n−4)→…→(1,1,1)(1,1,n−2)→(1,1,n−4)→…→(1,1,1)

The number of operations is the length of the sequence 1,3,5,7,…,2(⌊n2⌋−1)+11,3,5,7,…,2(⌊n2⌋−1)+1

By the representation of odd numbers we've 2(0)+1,2(1)1+,…,2(⌊n2⌋−1)+12(0)+1,2(1)1+,…,2(⌊n2⌋−1)+1 , which is equivalent to the length of 0,1,2,…,n−12−10,1,2,…,n−12−1 which gives the length of n−12n−12.

Both cases are equivalent to the close form

⌊n−12⌋⌊n−12⌋

Complexity O(1)O(1).

Implementation 1 (MathModel)
from math import *

def solve():
    n=int(input())
    print((n-1)//2)

for _ in range(int(input())):
    solve()
Solution 2

It's possible to use fair split that's we use ⌊n3⌋,⌊n3⌋,⌊n3⌋+nmod3⌊n3⌋,⌊n3⌋,⌊n3⌋+nmod3 and keep simulating with n=m3n=m3 on steps except the first step until you reach m2≤2m2≤2.

Complexity O(log3(n))O(log3⁡(n))

Implementation 2 (MathModel)
from math import *

def solve():
    n=int(input())
    a,b,c=n//3,n//3,n//3+(n%3)
    ans=0
    while(n>2):
        ans+=n//3
        n=n//3+(n%3)
    print(ans)

for _ in range(int(input())):
    solve()
Rate The Problem !

294 Great

92 Good

71 Neutral

22 Bad

15 Didn't Solve

2156B - Strange Machine

Hints
Hint 1

What's the consuming part of processing the string of operations ss ?

Hint 2

If the string ss contained at least one BB operation , What's the maximum count of iterations we're going to run so that a=0a=0 ?

Hint 3

If the string ss contains xx copies of BB then aa is guaranteed to be a=0a=0 in at most O(log2xA)O(log2x⁡A) where A=max(a1,a2,…,an)A=max(a1,a2,…,an) , that's at most O(nlog2A)∼O(30n)O(nlog2⁡A)∼O(30n) operations are required .

Hint 4

How to manage too many appearances of AA for efficiency ?

Hint 5

It's enough to handle the case where AAAAA…AAAAAAAA…AAA⏟ that's the answer for query aa is aa because we're incrementing by 1 at each iteration ; otherwise the complexity becomes O(109q)O(109q) for such cases which is slow. and the rest can be done in O(nqlog2(109))O(nqlog2⁡(109)) which is safe according to the constraints.

Solution 1 : O(nq log A)

If you've one BB in string ss , then you'll have to do operations in O(nlog2(A))O(nlog2⁡(A)) which is ∼O(20⋅30)∼O(20⋅30) which is fine for n≤20n≤20 and ∑q≤104∑q≤104 , at worst case this is roughly O(6⋅105)O(6⋅105) which is safe.

If we've a string ss that's all AA i.e. :

s=AAAAA…AAAs=AAAAA…AAA⏟

then answer is simply for each aiai is aiai itself because we cannot do anything except just subtracting by 11 many times ; otherwise the complexity becomes O(109q)O(109q) for such cases which is slow.

Complexity O(nqlog2(109))O(nqlog2⁡(109)).

Implementation 1 (MathModel)
def solve():
    n,q=map(int,input().split())
    s=input().strip()
    a=list(map(int,input().split()))
    A,B=s.count('A'),s.count('B')
    for i in range(q):
        if(B==0):
            print(a[i])
        else:
            ans=0
            while(a[i]):
                for j in s:
                    if(a[i]==0):break
                    ans+=1
                    if(j=='A'):a[i]-=1
                    else: a[i]//=2
            print(ans)

for _ in range(int(input())):
    solve()
Bonus Hints
Hint 1

Compress the string ss into sequence of "Move Blocks" that's for s=AABBBABAAAs=AABBBABAAA the sequence of moves will be

{[A,2],[B,3],[A,1],[B,1],[A,3]}{[A,2],[B,3],[A,1],[B,1],[A,3]}
Hint 2

Be careful in simulation

Solution 2 (Bonus) : O(n+qlog^2 A)

Observation : The consuming part of processing the operations where a segment of AAAAA…AAAAAAAA…AAA⏟ because we're processing one by one which is inefficient and answering query will take O(nqlog2(109))O(nqlog2⁡(109)) , why ? , because processing AAAAA…AAAAAAAA…AAA⏟ operation-by-operation is slow , for example consider the sequence AAAAA…AAABAAAAA…AAAB⏟

This case will take O(nqlog2(109))O(nqlog2⁡(109)) at worst case , which is inefficient.

A good way to optimize is by compress the blocks of same type of operations as (type,count)(type,count) that way all long blocks of AA will be performed much faster , For blocks of BB it's already fast to process them in order because of O(log2(a))O(log2⁡(a)) and it reduces aa to 00 in O(log2(109))∼30O(log2⁡(109))∼30 at most , However compressing the operations is still better to compose the operations.

That's we're going to process at most ∼60∼60 blocks , because if we started with ABABAB…ABABAB… assuming a=109a=109 , which is the worst case of the algorithm.

Now after composing the blocks as (type,count)(type,count) , you should iterate through them :

  • If si=Asi=A then we perform ai=ai−min(count,ai)ai=ai−min(count,ai)
  • If si=Bsi=B then we perform ai=⌊ai2min(count,⌈log2(n)⌉)⌋ai=⌊ai2min(count,⌈log2⁡(n)⌉)⌋

make sure to update number of operations accordingly.

Complexity ∼O(n+qlog2(109))∼O(n+qlog2⁡(109)).

Implementation 2 (MathModel)
def solve():
    n,q=map(int,input().split())
    s=input()
    a=list(map(int,input().split()))
    A=s.count('A')
    B=s.count('B')
    last=s[0]
    seq=[] 
    cur=1
    for i in range(1,n):
        if(s[i]==last): cur+=1
        else:
            seq.append([last,cur])
            cur=1
            last=s[i]
    if(cur!=0): seq.append([last,cur])
    if(B==0):
        for i in a: print(i)
    else:
        for i in range(q):
            ans=0
            while(a[i]>0):
                for j in seq:
                    if(a[i]==0): break
                    if(j[0]=='A'):
                        mn=min(j[1],a[i])
                        ans+=mn
                        a[i]-=mn
                    else:
                        if a[i]==0: break
                        x=a[i]
                        p=0
                        while(x):
                            x//=2
                            p+=1
                        mn=min(p,j[1])
                        ans+=mn
                        a[i]//=(2**mn)
            print(ans)

for _ in range(int(input())):
    solve()
Rate The Problem !

372 Great

109 Good

54 Neutral

49 Bad

43 Didn't Solve

2156C - Maximum GCD on Whiteboard

Hints
Hint 1

You can do all the Erase operations before the Split operations.

Proof

Suppose there exists an optimal solution where you do an Erase operation on an element that was produced from a Split operation. You can instead do an Erase operation on the original element that it was being Split from, which will result in the whiteboard containing a subset of the elements in the optimal solution. Hence, it will have a beauty of at least as much as the optimal solution.

Hint 2

Fix a target gcd gg. Which numbers can be made divisible by gg using the Split operation?

Hint 3

If xx is a multiple of gg, we do not need any Split operation. If x≥4gx≥4g, one split can result in two multiples of gg (discard the middle).

Proof

Split xx into (g,g+xmodg,x−2g−xmodg)(g,g+xmodg,x−2g−xmodg). Both gg and x−2g−xmodgx−2g−xmodg are divisible by gg.

  • If x<5gx<5g, x−2g−xmodg=2gx−2g−xmodg=2g. g+xmodg≤2g=x−2g−xmodgg+xmodg≤2g=x−2g−xmodg, hence, x1≤x2≤x3x1≤x2≤x3 is satisfied.
  • Otherwise, x≥5gx≥5g. x−2g−xmodg≥5g−2g−g=2g≥g+xmodgx−2g−xmodg≥5g−2g−g=2g≥g+xmodg, hence, x1≤x2≤x3x1≤x2≤x3 is satisfied.
Hint 4

Conversely, if x<4gx<4g and xx is not divisible by gg, you can’t make it good by splits → it must be erased.

Proof

We will prove this by strong induction.

Let PiPi be the statement: ii cannot be made divisible by gg using any number of splits if i<4gi<4g and ii is not divisible by gg.

Base case: P1P1 is true as 11 cannot be split.

Suppose PiPi is true for all i<Ki<K for some K≥2K≥2. We will prove that PKPK is also true.

If K≥4gK≥4g or KK is divisible by gg, PKPK is vacuously true. From here on, we will assume that K<4gK<4g and KK is not divisible by gg.

Suppose we split KK into x1≤x2≤x3<Kx1≤x2≤x3<K. If either x1x1 or x3x3 is not divisible by gg, Px1Px1 and Px3Px3 implies that they can never be made divisible by gg using any number of splits. Hence, if we want to make KK divisible by gg, x1x1 and x3x3 have to both be divisible by gg.

If x1≥2gx1≥2g, x3≥x2≥x1≥2gx3≥x2≥x1≥2g, which means that K=x1+x2+x3≥2g+2g+2g=6gK=x1+x2+x3≥2g+2g+2g=6g, which contradicts K<4gK<4g. Hence, x1=gx1=g is the only possibility.

  • If x3=gx3=g, x1≤x2≤x3→g≤x2≤gx1≤x2≤x3→g≤x2≤g. Hence, x2=gx2=g as well, and K=x1+x2+x3=g+g+g=3gK=x1+x2+x3=g+g+g=3g, contradicting that KK is not divisible by gg.
  • If x3≥2gx3≥2g, K=x1+x2+x3≤g+g+2g=4gK=x1+x2+x3≤g+g+2g=4g, contradicting K<4gK<4g.

Hence, it is not posible to make KK divisible by gg using any number of splits.

Hint 5

So gg is good if

#{x≥4g}+#{x=g}+#{x=2g}+#{x=3g} ≥ n−k.#{x≥4g}+#{x=g}+#{x=2g}+#{x=3g} ≥ n−k.
Hint 6

Count with a frequency array / map and a prefix sum for g=1,2,…,ng=1,2,…,n

Solution

We want the maximum gcd gg achievable after ≤k≤k erases and any splits.

For a fixed gg, the elements that won’t consume an erase are:

  • all multiples of gg (already good), and
  • any x≥4gx≥4g (can be made good via one split using the construction in hint 3).

Thus, the only "bad" numbers (that would have to be erased) are those with x<4gx<4g that are not multiples of gg. Therefore gg is good if

good(g)=#{x≥4g}+#{x=g}+#{x=2g}+#{x=3g} ≥ n−k.good(g)=#{x≥4g}+#{x=g}+#{x=2g}+#{x=3g} ≥ n−k.

Since 1≤ai≤n1≤ai≤n, precompute a frequency array and prefix sums p[v]=∑u≤vf[u]p[v]=∑u≤vf[u]. Then

#{x≥4g}=n−p[min(n,4g−1)].#{x≥4g}=n−p[min(n,4g−1)].

Iterate through g=1,2,3,…,ng=1,2,3,…,n and keep the largest good gg.

Complexity : O(n)O(n).

Implementation (MathModel)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long

void solve(){
  int n,k;cin>>n>>k;
  vector<int>a(n),mp(n+1),p(n+1);
  for(auto &i:a){
     cin>>i;
     mp[i]++;
  }
  for(int i=1;i<=n;i++){
      p[i]=p[i-1]+mp[i];
  }
  int ans=1;
  for(int g=1;g<=n;g++){
    int t=min(n,4*g-1);
    int good=n-p[t];
    if(g<=n)good+=mp[g];
    if(2*g<=n)good+=mp[2*g];
    if(3*g<=n)good+=mp[3*g];
    if(good>=n-k)ans=g;
  }
  cout<<ans<<"\n";
}

signed main(){
  ios::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  int t;cin>>t;
  while(t--)solve();
}
Rate The Problem !

660 Great

54 Good

20 Neutral

43 Bad

181 Didn't Solve

2156D - Find the Last Number

Hints
Hint 1

We determine the kk-th bit of pnpn for k=0,1,…,⌊log2n⌋k=0,1,…,⌊log2⁡n⌋.

Hint 2

Let bitk(i)bitk(i) denote the whether the kk-th bit of ii is zero or one. How can we determine the kk-th bit of pnpn, i.e. bitk(pn)bitk(pn), from bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1)bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1)?

Hint 3
∑⌊log2(n)⌋k=0⌈n−12k⌉=n−1+⌈n−12⌉+⌈n−14⌉+⌈n−18⌉+…<2n∑k=0⌊log2⁡(n)⌋⌈n−12k⌉=n−1+⌈n−12⌉+⌈n−14⌉+⌈n−18⌉+…<2n

How can we achieve this halving?

Solution

Recall from Hint 2 that bitk(i)bitk(i) is defined as kk-th bit of the integer ii. A naive solution using nlog2nnlog2⁡n queries is as follows:

  • Compute bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1)bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1) for all k=0,1,…,⌊log2n⌋k=0,1,…,⌊log2⁡n⌋.

    • bitk(pi)bitk(pi) can be computed with a single query pi AND 2kpi AND 2k (1≤i≤n−11≤i≤n−1).

  • If bitk(1)+bitk(2)+…+bitk(n)=bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1)bitk(1)+bitk(2)+…+bitk(n)=bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1), it means that the kk-th bit of pnpn is zero. Otherwise, it is one.

    • This works due to the fact that bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1)=bitk(1)+bitk(2)+…+bitk(n)−bitk(pn)bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1)=bitk(1)+bitk(2)+…+bitk(n)−bitk(pn).

To optimise this, we will half the search space after each step. Starting from k=0k=0, we still use n−1n−1 queries to calculate bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1)bitk(p1)+bitk(p2)+…+bitk(pn−1). After using this to determine the value of the 00-th bit of pnpn, we can discard all pipi that has a different 00-th bit from pnpn.

More formally, let S0S0 denote the set of elements ii such that ii has the same 00-th bit as pnpn, and P0P0 denote the set of elements ii (1≤i≤n−11≤i≤n−1) such that pipi has the same 00-th bit as pnpn. ∑i∈P0bit1(pi)=(∑i∈S0bit1(i))−bit1(pn)∑i∈P0bit1(pi)=(∑i∈S0bit1(i))−bit1(pn), so the 11-st bit of pnpn is zero if ∑i∈P0bit1(pi)=∑i∈S0bit1(i)∑i∈P0bit1(pi)=∑i∈S0bit1(i).

In general, we can let SkSk denote the set of elements ii such that ii has the same 00-th, 11-st, …… , kk-th bit as pnpn, and PkPk denote the set of elements ii (1≤i≤n−11≤i≤n−1) such that pipi has the same 00-th, 11-st, …… , kk-th bit as pnpn. Then, the kk-th bit of pnpn is zero if ∑i∈Pk−1bitk(pi)=∑i∈Sk−1bitk(i)∑i∈Pk−1bitk(pi)=∑i∈Sk−1bitk(i).

Observe that |Pk|≤⌈|Pk−1|2⌉|Pk|≤⌈|Pk−1|2⌉ for all k≥1k≥1. Hence, the total number of queries used is

n−1+∑⌊log2(n)⌋k=1|Pk−1|≤n−1+⌈n−12⌉+⌈n−14⌉+⌈n−18⌉+…<2nn−1+∑k=1⌊log2⁡(n)⌋|Pk−1|≤n−1+⌈n−12⌉+⌈n−14⌉+⌈n−18⌉+…<2n
Implementation (hxu10)
def query(index,num):
    print("?",index,num)
    return int(input())

def main():
    n = int(input())
    rest = set()
    likely = set()
    for i in range(1,n):
        rest.add(i)
    for i in range(1,n+1):
        likely.add(i)
    D = len(bin(n)[2:])
    ans = 0
    for d in range(D):
        index_0 = set()
        index_1 = set() 
        likely_0 = set()
        likely_1 = set()
        for index in rest:
            res = query(index, 1<<d)   
            if res == 0:
                index_0.add(index)
            else:
                index_1.add(index)  
        zeros = 0
        ones = 0  
        for num in likely:
            if num & (1<<d):
                ones += 1
                likely_1.add(num)
            else:
                zeros += 1
                likely_0.add(num)
        if zeros == len(index_0):
            ans += 1 << d
            rest = index_1
            likely = likely_1
        elif ones == len(index_1):
            rest = index_0
            likely = likely_0
        else:
            print("Error")
            return 
    print("!",ans)

T = int(input())
t = 1
while t <= T:
    main()
    t += 1
Rate The Problem !

359 Great

35 Good

7 Neutral

33 Bad

64 Didn't Solve

2156E - Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

Hints
Hint 1

Consider the simplified version of the game where Alex moves first.

Hint 1.1

Suppose Alex wants to ensure a beauty of at least gg (the goal), while Hao tries to keep it below gg. Which indices should Alex lock to achieve this goal?

Hint 1.2

We can binary search on the goal gg.

Hint 1.3

For a given gg and an index ii, we define fg(a,i)fg(a,i) as the number of indices j≠ij≠i satisfying amax(i,j)−amin(i,j)≥gamax(i,j)−amin(i,j)≥g.

If there exists an index ii such that fg(a,i)≥2fg(a,i)≥2, then Alex can lock index ii on the first move. No matter which index Hao removes next, there will still exist at least one jj such that |ai−aj|≥g|ai−aj|≥g. Thus, Alex can secure that pair and guarantee a beauty of at least gg.

Hint 1.4

If fg(a,i)≤1fg(a,i)≤1 for all indices ii, Alex cannot achieve his goal. Whichever index ii Alex locks first, Hao can remove the single jj such that |ai−aj|≥g|ai−aj|≥g. Hence, Alex will never be able to lock two indices with a difference ≥g≥g, and the resulting beauty will be strictly less than gg.

Hint 2

Now return to the original problem, where Hao moves first.

To keep the final beauty below gg, Hao must remove an index such that after the removal, fg(a,i)<2fg(a,i)<2 for all ii. Otherwise, Alex will have a winning move as shown in Hint 1.3.

Hint 3

For each ii, we only need to track whether fg(a,i)fg(a,i) is 00, 11, 22, or greater than 22. This is because if there are any index ii with fg(a,i)>2fg(a,i)>2, Hao has to remove index ii on his first move. Otherwise, we will have fg(a,i)≥2fg(a,i)≥2 after Hao's first move, allowing Alex to have a beauty of at least gg as mentioned in Hint 2.

This observation allows us to compute fg(a,i)fg(a,i) efficiently.

Solution

Read the above hints before continuing.

As noted in Hint 1.2, we can binary search on the target beauty gg.

For each index 1≤i≤n1≤i≤n, recall that fg(a,i)fg(a,i) is the number of indices j≠ij≠i satisfying amax(i,j)−amin(i,j)≥gamax(i,j)−amin(i,j)≥g.

As mentioned in Hint 3, we only care whether fg(a,i)fg(a,i) is 00, 11, 22, or greater than 22. We iterate through i=1,…,ni=1,…,n, and maintain the three smallest previous values ajaj (for j<ij<i). For each of these, we check whether the condition holds and increment both fg(a,i)fg(a,i) and fg(a,j)fg(a,j) if it does. This leads to an O(3n)=O(n)O(3n)=O(n) computation per gg.

We simulate each possible first move pp by Hao. Let array bb be array aa after removing index pp. When Hao removes index pp, only the fg(a,p)fg(a,p) indices qq satisfying |ap−aq|≥g|ap−aq|≥g will have their fg(b,q)=fg(a,q)−1fg(b,q)=fg(a,q)−1, while all others remain unchanged.

Hao succeeds in keeping the beauty below gg if and only if removing index pp results in all fg(b,i)≤1fg(b,i)≤1. Equivalently, the indices qq paired with pp must include all indices that initially had fg(a,i)=2fg(a,i)=2.

If no such removal pp works, Alex can achieve his goal of keeping the beauty at least gg.

Checking each first move pp requires O(fg(a,p))O(fg(a,p)) time, and summing up over ∑1≤p≤nfg(a,p)≤2(3n)=O(n)∑1≤p≤nfg(a,p)≤2(3n)=O(n) since we only maintain at most three previous values of ajaj (for j<ij<i) during the calculation of fg(a,i)fg(a,i).

The total time complexity is O(nlog2109)O(nlog2⁡109) since we perform a binary search over gg and compute fg(a,i)fg(a,i) in linear time per iteration.

Implementation (hxu,PyPy)
def main():
    n = int(input())
    arr = list(map(int,input().split()))

    # calculate the number of j for each i that satisfy arr[max(i,j)] - arr[min(i,j)] >= target
    def measurecount(arr,target):
        m = len(arr)
        count = [0]*m
        minimum = []
        for i in range(m):
            for j in range(len(minimum)):
                if minimum[j] + target <= arr[i]:
                    count[i] += 1
            minimum.append(arr[i])
            minimum.sort()
            if len(minimum) > 3:
                minimum.pop()
        maximum = []
        for i in range(m-1,-1,-1):
            for j in range(len(maximum)):
                if maximum[j] - target >= arr[i]:
                    count[i] += 1
            maximum.append(arr[i])
            maximum.sort(reverse=True)
            if len(maximum) > 3:
                maximum.pop()
        return count

    # binary search to judge if we can find such pair
    def judge(target):
        count = measurecount(arr,target)
        if max(count) <= 1:
            return False
        pick = []
        if max(count) > 2:
            for i in range(n):
                if count[i] > 2:
                    pick = [i]
                    break
        else:
            for i in range(n):
                if count[i] != 2: 
                    continue 
                for j in range(n):
                    if j==i: 
                        pick.append(j)
                    elif j < i and arr[j] + target <= arr[i]: 
                        pick.append(j)
                    elif j > i and arr[i] + target <= arr[j]:
                        pick.append(j)
                break

        for p in pick:
            count2 = measurecount(arr[:p]+arr[p+1:], target)
            if max(count2) <= 1:
                return False
        return True       

    front = -10**9 - 10
    rear = 10**9 + 10
    while front < rear:
        mid = front + (rear-front)//2
        res = judge(mid)
        if res:
            front = mid + 1
        else:
            rear = mid 
    ans = front - 1
    print(ans)
                       
T = int(input())
t = 1
while t <= T:
    main()
    t += 1
Implementation (maomao90,C++)
#include "bits/stdc++.h"
using namespace std;
 
const int MAXN = 200005;
const int INF = 1000000005;
 
inline int fdiv(int num, int denom) {
  return num / denom - ((num ^ denom) < 0 && num % denom);
}
inline int cdiv(int num, int denom) { return fdiv(num + denom - 1, denom); }
 
int t;
int n;
int a[MAXN];
 
vector<int> adj[MAXN];
void add_edge(int u, int v) {
  adj[u].push_back(v);
  adj[v].push_back(u);
}
bool check(int x) {
  for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    adj[i].clear();
  }
  set<pair<int, int>> st;
  for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    int cnt = 0;
    for (auto [prv, prv_id] : st) {
      if (a[i] - prv >= x) {
        add_edge(prv_id, i);
      }
    }
    st.insert({a[i], i});
    if ((int) st.size() > 3) {
        st.erase(prev(st.end()));
    }
  }
 
  int bad_cnt = 0;
  for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    if ((int)adj[i].size() >= 2) {
      bad_cnt++;
    }
  }
  for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    int delta = (int)adj[i].size() >= 2;
    for (int v : adj[i]) {
      if (adj[v].size() == 2) {
        delta++;
      }
    }
    if (delta == bad_cnt) {
      return false;
    }
  }
  return true;
}
 
int main() {
  cin >> t;
  while (t--) {
    cin >> n;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
      cin >> a[i];
    }
    int lo = -INF, hi = INF;
    while (lo < hi) {
      int mid = cdiv(lo + hi, 2);
      if (check(mid)) {
        lo = mid;
      } else {
        hi = mid - 1;
      }
    }
    cout << lo << '\n';
  }
  return 0;
}
Rate The Problem !

102 Great

13 Good

3 Neutral

10 Bad

49 Didn't Solve

2156F1 - Strange Operation (Easy Version)

hxu10's Solution
Hints
Hint 1

What will the first element of permutation be for the final result?

Hint 2

If the first element is odd, we can always reduce it to 11, otherwise, we can always reduce it to 22.

Hint 3

If the first element is even, before reducing the first element to 22, we must reduce some element to 11 at first. Which one to choose?

Hint 4

You should choose the leftmost odd elements that all elements to the left is greater than it.

Hint 5

Treat the rest of elements as a new permutation

Solution

Since we need to find the lexicographically smallest permutation, our goal is to make the numbers on the left as small as possible.

Suppose we have a permutation [p1,p2,…,pn][p1,p2,…,pn].
The first question we would like to answer is: where will the number 11 appear in the final lexicographically smallest permutation?

Case 1: p1p1 is odd.

In this case, the answer is straightforward — p1p1 can always be decreased to 11. Hence, 11 will appear at the first position.

Case 2: p1p1 is even.

This case is more complicated. For a number pipi to be reduced to 11, two conditions must hold:

  1. pipi is odd.
  2. All numbers 1,2,…,pi−11,2,…,pi−1 appear to the right of index ii.
    In other words, for every j<ij<i, we must have pj>pipj>pi.

We find the minimum possible index ii that satisfies both of the above condition, and reduce pipi to 11.
After that, since p1p1 is even, we reduce p1p1 to 22.

It can be proven that choosing the smallest such index ii and performing these two reductions, first decreasing pipi to 11, then decreasing p1p1 to 22, will always yield the lexicographically smallest permutation. A formal proof will be given later.

Recursive construction:

  • If p1p1 is odd, reducing pipi to 11 transforms the remaining multiset [p2,p3,…,pn][p2,p3,…,pn] into [2,3,…,n][2,3,…,n]. This can be treated as a new permutation problem of size n−1n−1.

  • If p1p1 is even, after reducing pipi to 11 and p1p1 to 22,
    the remaining multiset [p2,p3,…,pi−1,pi+1,…,pn][p2,p3,…,pi−1,pi+1,…,pn] becomes [3,4,…,n][3,4,…,n], which again forms a smaller permutation problem of size n−2n−2.

Thus, the process can be applied recursively.

Complexity Analysis:

The overall time complexity is O(n2)O(n2), which is efficient enough to pass the first subtask (F1).

Proof

Now let's prove that this solution always lead the permutation to smallest lexigraphically possible.

Suppose we have permutation [p1,p2,…,pn][p1,p2,…,pn]

When p1p1 is odd, the conclusion is straightforward. We can always decrease p1p1 to 11 and treat the remaining elements as a new permutation.

When p1p1 is even, let ii be the minimum possible index such that pipi can be decreased to 11. In other words, ii is the smallest index such that min(p1,p2,…,pi−1)>pimin(p1,p2,…,pi−1)>pi and pipi is odd. We need to prove that decreasing pipi (1<i≤n1<i≤n) to 11 does not affect the lexicographically smallest possible prefix [p1,p2,…,pi−1][p1,p2,…,pi−1] that can be formed.

Transforming [p1,p2,…,pi−1][p1,p2,…,pi−1] into its lexicographically smallest possible form involves three types of operations. Suppose an operation chooses indices x<y<zx<y<z and performs the following steps: 1) item subtract 22 from pxpx, 2) add 11 to pypy, and 3) add 11 to pzpz.

We analyze each possible case relative to ii:

Case 1: x<y<z<ix<y<z<i

  • This case is trivial. Changing pipi to 11 only affects indices greater than ii, so these operations remain completely valid.

Case 2: x<y<i<zx<y<i<z

  • Suppose pzpz is within the subarray [pi,…,pn][pi,…,pn]. After changing pipi to 11, the element pzpz (or its value) still exists, perhaps at a different position, but the multiset of values in [pi,…,pn][pi,…,pn] remains unchanged. Therefore, the operation can still be performed.

Case 3: x<i<y<zx<i<y<z

  • Since the operation does not depend on the relative order or values of pypy and pzpz, and the multiset [pi,…,pn][pi,…,pn] is preserved, we can still find suitable pypy and pzpz to perform the operation. Hence, this case also remains valid.

Case 4: y=iy=i or z=iz=i

  • If pi>1pi>1, then after decreasing pipi to 11, there must exist some index j>ij>i such that pjpj equals the original value of pipi. Thus, the operation can still be performed using pjpj.

  • If pi=1pi=1, then it is impossible for any j<ij<i to have pj=1pj=1, as that would violate the rule of minimality. This is precisely why we must choose the smallest possible index ii for which pipi can be reduced to 11; otherwise, the prefix could incorrectly contain 11.

Therefore, if there exists a sequence of operations that transforms [p1,p2,…,pi−1][p1,p2,…,pi−1] into the lexicographically smallest possible prefix, none of these operations will be affected by decreasing pipi to 11 beforehand.

Hence, we can always decrease pipi to 11 without affecting the lexicographically smallest prefix.

Implementation (PyPy)
def main():
    n = int(input())
    p = list(map(int,input().split()))
    for i in range(n):
        p[i] -= 1
    seq = []
    indexes = [i for i in range(n)]
    def update(indexes):
        count = 0
        for i in range(n):
            if indexes[i] < 0:  continue
            indexes[i] = count
            count += 1
    curr = 1
    ans = [-1]*n
    for i in range(n):
        if ans[i] > 0:  
            continue
        if indexes[p[i]] % 2 == 0:
            ans[i] = curr
            curr += 1
            indexes[p[i]] = -1
            update(indexes)
            continue 
        prefix = p[i]
        pick = -1
        for j in range(i+1,n):
            if ans[j] > 0:
                continue 
            if p[j] > prefix or indexes[p[j]] % 2 > 0:
                prefix = min(prefix,p[j])
                continue
            pick = j 
            break
        ans[j] = curr 
        curr += 1
        ans[i] = curr
        curr += 1
        indexes[p[j]] = -1
        indexes[p[i]] = -1
        update(indexes)
    print(*ans)      
T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    main()
Tester's Solution
Hints
Hint 1

Each move only shuffles the triple of consecutive values a,a+1,a+2a,a+1,a+2 and places the smallest among them at the leftmost of the three chosen positions.

Hint 2

Think in terms of a value’s rank among the not-yet-fixed values: one move on a value is like moving it two places to the left in that order. A value at rank rr can move at most ⌊r/2⌋⌊r/2⌋ steps.

Hint 3

To minimize lexicographically, process positions from left to right and push the current choice as far left as allowed. Parity matters: when the current rank is odd, you must “pair” with a smaller value to make the plan realizable.

Solution

We want the lexicographically smallest permutation reachable by the triple move. Focus on the first position :

  • If the first value is odd, we can keep applying moves that reduce it by 22 until it becomes 11.
  • If it is even, we can keep reducing by 22 until it becomes 22.

Why? Each move that subtracts 22 from some position ii uses two positions j,kj,k to its right that currently hold the two consecutive values just below it. In a permutation these exist until you reach 11 (odd start) or 22 (even start). Using them only increases those right-side values by 11, so they never block making the first value as small as parity allows.

Now suppose the first value is even and we set it to 22. The next question is: where should 11 go? The correct choice is the leftmost value that can be reduced to 11.

Two simple facts characterize when a value vv can be reduced to a target cntcnt:

  1. Parity: moves change a value by ±2±2 overall, so v≡cnt(mod2)v≡cnt(mod2) is necessary.
  2. No smaller blockers to the left: to reduce vv down step by step, for each intermediate step you must pick the pair x,x+1x,x+1 with x∈cnt,cnt+1,…,v−1x∈cnt,cnt+1,…,v−1 to the right of vv. So vv must lie to the left of all values in [cnt,v−1][cnt,v−1].

This gives a clean greedy: at step cnt=1,2,…,ncnt=1,2,…,n, among values i∈[cnt…n]i∈[cnt…n], scan by increasing ii and keep the running minimum position seen so far. Every time that minimum improves (a “record minimum”), if i≡cnt(mod2)i≡cnt(mod2) then value ii is feasible to reduce to cntcnt; among all feasible ones, the final record minimum is leftmost and is the one we must take.

Why lexicographically optimal? The array first differs where we place cntcnt. Picking the leftmost feasible position ensures the smallest possible number at that index. Any alternative that puts cntcnt strictly to the right makes the prefix strictly larger and cannot be beaten later.

After choosing the index cur where we install cntcnt, we conceptually “pull down” the current value val=p[cur]val=p[cur] to cntcnt by a chain of moves. Each such move also raises exactly one of the values in cnt,…,val−1cnt,…,val−1 by 11. Over the whole chain, every number in cnt,…,val−1cnt,…,val−1 gets raised exactly once. So, in one shot, we can set p[cur]=cntp[cur]=cnt and increase all values in [cnt…val−1][cnt…val−1] by 11. The remaining numbers still form a permutation of cnt+1,…,ncnt+1,…,n, and we repeat with cnt+1cnt+1.

Because each step is just a linear scan over values and a few local updates, this runs in O(n2)O(n2) per test.

Implementation (C++)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <numeric>
#include <tuple>
#include <map>
#include <set>
#include <deque>
#include <queue>
#include <functional>
#include <cassert>
#include <immintrin.h>

using namespace std;

#define int long long

int p[100005];
int pos[100005];

signed main()
{
#if _LOCAL_
    freopen("case.txt", "r", stdin);
#endif

    int t;
    cin >> t;

    while(t--) {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            cin >> p[i];
        }
        for(int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            pos[p[i]] = i;
        }
        for(int cnt = 1; cnt <= n; ++cnt) {
            int cur = n;
            for(int i = cnt, mn = n; i <= n; ++i) {
                if(pos[i] < mn) {
                    mn = pos[i];
                    if((i & 1) == (cnt & 1)) {
                        cur = pos[i];
                    }
                }
            }
            int val = p[cur];
            p[cur] = cnt;
            for(int i = cnt; i < val; ++i) {
                p[pos[i]]++;
            }
            for(int i = val; i > cnt; --i) {
                pos[i] = pos[i-1];
            }
            pos[cnt] = cur;
        }
        for(int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            cout << p[i] << ' ';
        }
        cout << endl;
    }

    return 0;
}
MathModel's Solution (Bonus/Challenge)
Hints
Hint 1

A move is only possible on three consecutive values: there must exist an mm such that the values involved are exactly m,m+1,m+2m,m+1,m+2 at indices i<j<ki<j<k.

Hint 2

pi=max(pj,pk)+1pi=max(pj,pk)+1 and pi=min(pj,pk)+2pi=min(pj,pk)+2

Those equations force the three values to be consecutive and the largest to sit at the leftmost index, which is exactly c<min(a,b)c<min(a,b) with values m,m+1,m+2m,m+1,m+2.

Hint 3

Each move strictly decreases lexicographic order: the first changed index is i=ci=c, whose value drops from m+2m+2 to mm; earlier indices remain unchanged.

Hint 4

Each move removes at least two inversions among the triple (and never adds inversions before ii), so the process is acyclic and must terminate.

Hint 5

Number of operations is ≤O(n2)≤O(n2) (each removes ≥2≥2 inversions). The pointer-walk advances by +1+1 when no operations, and jumps back by at most 22 per op. Total steps are O(n+ops)=O(n2)O(n+ops)=O(n2) overall.

Solution

Please Read Hints

Maintain pos[v]pos[v]. Iterate mm from 00 to n−3n−3:

  • Let
a=pos[m],b=pos[m+1],c=pos[m+2].a=pos[m],b=pos[m+1],c=pos[m+2].

  • If c<min(a,b)c<min(a,b), apply the operation on indices a,b,ca,b,c:

    • Set i=ci=c, j=min(a,b)j=min(a,b), k=max(a,b)k=max(a,b).
    • If a<ba<b, write (m,m+1,m+2)(m,m+1,m+2) to (i,j,k)(i,j,k) and set (pos[m],pos[m+1],pos[m+2])=(i,j,k)(pos[m],pos[m+1],pos[m+2])=(i,j,k).
    • Else write (m,m+2,m+1)(m,m+2,m+1) to (i,j,k)(i,j,k) and set (pos[m],pos[m+2],pos[m+1])=(i,j,k)(pos[m],pos[m+2],pos[m+1])=(i,j,k).
    • Then set m←max(m−2,0)m←max(m−2,0).
  • Otherwise, set m←m+1m←m+1.

This process terminates when m=n−2m=n−2, at which point every triple satisfies pos[m+2]≥min(pos[m],pos[m+1])pos[m+2]≥min(pos[m],pos[m+1]), so no legal move exists anywhere. Because each step is lexicographically decreasing (when an operation is performed) and the space of permutations is finite and acyclic under these moves, the result is the lexicographically smallest reachable permutation. The pointer-walk guarantees total time O(n2)O(n2).

Challenge: Can you prove the solution?

Implementation
for _ in range(int(input())):
    n=int(input())
    p=list(map(int,input().split()))
    pos=[0]*n
    for i in range(n):
        pos[p[i]-1]=i
    ch=1
    while ch:
        ch=0
        for m in range(n-2):
            a,b,c=pos[m],pos[m+1],pos[m+2]
            if(c<min(a,b)):
                i=c
                if(a<b):
                    j,k=a,b
                    p[i],p[j],p[k]=m+1,m+2,m+3
                    pos[m],pos[m+1],pos[m+2]=i,j,k
                else:
                    j,k=b,a
                    p[i],p[j],p[k]=m+1,m+3,m+2
                    pos[m],pos[m+2],pos[m+1]=i,j,k
                ch=1
    print(*p)
Rate The Problem !

56 Great

8 Good

8 Neutral

64 Bad

28 Didn't Solve

2156F2 - Strange Operation (Hard Version)

Hints
Hint 1

Suppose the operation changes indices i,j,ki,j,k, other than ii, all the other indices relative position doesn't change.

Hint 2

Use segment tree and fenwick tree

Hint 3

Iterate from 11 to nn, during the tt-th iteration, find the leftmost element that can be decreased by tt.

Hint 4

Acclerate the procedure by finding next smaller element.

Solution

You should Read Editorial of F1 First

Let the initial permutation be p=(p1,p2,…,pn)p=(p1,p2,…,pn), and let the final array be q=(q1,q2,…,qn)q=(q1,q2,…,qn). Initialize qi=0qi=0 for all ii, meaning every position in the output is still undetermined. During the tt-th iteration, find the smallest index ii whose value can be decreased to tt. Repeatedly choose this ii, locate indices j,kj,k such that pi=max(pj,pk)+1pi=max(pj,pk)+1 and pi=min(pj,pk)+2pi=min(pj,pk)+2, and perform the updates pi←pi−2pi←pi−2, pj←pj+1pj←pj+1, pk←pk+1pk←pk+1. This is equivalent to removing pipi and setting qi←tqi←t. After removing pipi, the remaining elements p1,…,pi−1,pi+1,…,pnp1,…,pi−1,pi+1,…,pn close the gap (preserving relative order), forming a permutation of length n−1n−1.

This remove-and-assign simulation can be supported by a Fenwick tree or a segment tree so that each update and query runs in O(logn)O(log⁡n) time; however, naively finding the correct index ii could still cost O(n)O(n) per step. Define the rank of pipi as the number of alive (not yet removed) elements that are not greater than pipi, including pipi itself.

To speed up the process, keep a pointer at the smallest alive index ii and check its rank. If the rank is odd, remove this element immediately. If the rank is even, move the pointer to the next smaller element to its right, that is, to the smallest j>ij>i with pj<pipj<pi. Continue until you reach an element with odd rank.

Although a single "move to the next smaller on the right" step may look linear, the total number of such moves over the whole algorithm is at most 2n2n. If an element is first visited when its rank is even, after some element to its right is removed, its rank becomes odd the next time it is visited; if an element’s rank is already odd, it is removed immediately upon visit. Therefore each index is visited at most twice.

To implement this, we maintain two data structures:

  1. A Fenwick tree over values, where a 11 at position pipi indicates that the value pipi is still alive (and 00 otherwise). This lets you query the current rank of pipi in O(logn)O(log⁡n) time.

  2. Let ipip denote the array that is the inverse of pp, i.e., ippi=iippi=i. Store a range-min segment tree over the array ipip . When the value pipi is removed, set ippi=∞ippi=∞. If the current visited index is ii, then a range-min query on [1,pi−1][1,pi−1] returns the minimum position jj such that j>ij>i and pj<pipj<pi (the next smaller element on the right by value). Each query and update costs O(logn)O(log⁡n), and each index is examined at most twice, so the overall complexity is O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n), which is sufficient for the F2 constraints.

Implementation (hxu10,PyPy)
inf = 10**9

class fenwick(object):
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.n = n
        self.cul = [0]*n
        self.maxd = len(bin(n))-3
    def update(self,index,diff):
        i = index
        while i<self.n:
            self.cul[i] += diff
            i += (i+1)&(-i-1)
    def getaccu(self,index):
        output = 0
        i = index
        while i>=0:
            output += self.cul[i]
            i -= (i+1)&(-i-1)
        return output

class segment_tree(object):
    def merge(self,num,accu):
        return min(accu,num)
    def __init__(self,n,initial):
        self.n = n
        self.inf = inf
        self.arr = [self.inf]*(2*n)
        for i in range(2*n-1,0,-1):
            if i>=n:  self.arr[i] = initial[i-n]
            else:     self.arr[i] = self.merge(self.arr[2*i],self.arr[2*i+1])

    def update(self,index,target):
        index += self.n
        self.arr[index] = target
        while index > 0:
            if index & 1:
                nexttarget = self.merge( self.arr[index], self.arr[index-1])
            else:
                nexttarget = self.merge( self.arr[index], self.arr[index+1])
            self.arr[index>>1] = nexttarget
            index = index >> 1

    def addnum(self,index,diff):
        self.update(index, self.arr[index+self.n] + diff)

    def query(self,left,right):
        i,j = self.n+left,  self.n+right+1
        output = self.inf  # initial output should be changed if you want to change the merge function
        while i<j:
            if i&1:
                output = self.merge(self.arr[i],output)
                i += 1
            if j&1:
                j -= 1
                output = self.merge(self.arr[j],output)
            i = i >> 1
            j = j >> 1
        return output

def main(t):
    n = int(input())
    p = list(map(int,input().split()))
    location = [0]*n
    for i in range(n):
        p[i] -= 1
        location[p[i]] = i 
    seg = segment_tree(n, location)
    fen = fenwick(n)
    for i in range(n):
        fen.update(i,1)
    ans = [-1]*n
    curr = 1
    for i in range(n):
        if ans[i] >= 0:  
            continue 
        index = fen.getaccu(p[i]) - 1
        if index % 2 == 0:
            fen.update(p[i],-1)
            seg.update(p[i],inf)
            ans[i] = curr 
            curr += 1
            continue
        j = i 
        while True:
            j = seg.query(0,p[j]-1) 
            nextindex = fen.getaccu(p[j]) - 1
            if nextindex % 2 > 0:
                continue 
            break
        ans[j] = curr 
        ans[i] = curr + 1
        curr += 2
        seg.update(p[i],inf)
        seg.update(p[j],inf)
        fen.update(p[i],-1)
        fen.update(p[j],-1)
    print(*ans)

T = int(input())
for t in range(T):
    main(t)
Rate The Problem !

25 Great

3 Good

0 Neutral

4 Bad

41 Didn't Solve

Tutorial of Codeforces Round 1061 (Div. 2)

how you guys find that [2*i,3*i,4*i] thing in C during contest? still cant arrive at that.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    ASH9374
    5 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    ASH9374
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +1

    for a number greater than equal to 4i ( say q*i + r, where q>=4, 0<=r<i ) split to i,i+r,(q-2)*i and so i+r will be removed ( holding the inequality i <= i+r < (q-2)*i )

    if a number is less than 4*i then you cant split it such that their gcd will be i so the number themselves have to be divisible by i

    → Reply

    if g is the gcd you are considering: a number x which is n*g + r can always be split to g, g+r, (n-2)*g, but for g<g+r<(n-2)*g to hold, the number should be at least 4*g so we can split it to g,g+r,2*g otherwise it wont hold

← Rev. 8 →  
+3

Why Binary search is required for E — Hint 1.2 version ? (easier version where Alex moves first ? ).

Can't we just maintain two values for each index... primaryMin,secondaryMin on prefix,,, and primaryMax,secondaryMax in suffix.

Now, we assume, we are locking current index i as our first index (As alex)... so in next move Hao will try to delete primaryMax on right(suffix), or primaryMin on left(prefix), and we might get answer in O (N). ( loop i from 1 to N ). We will pick the index, which gives highest return.

Anything wrong with this approach ??????

cc : Editorialists ( maomao90 , MathModel ).

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    JeanBombeur
    5 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    JeanBombeur
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +8

    You can do it without binary search (which I did), although it takes a bit more work :

    For each index ii, find the 3 best targets j1,j2,j3j1,j2,j3, which give results a1⩾a2⩾a3a1⩾a2⩾a3. Now if Hao wants to deny the result a3a3, he can only do so by removing ii in the first move. If he want to deny a2a2, he can do so only by removing either ii or one of j1,j2j1,j2 in the first move. He can always deny a1a1.

    So sort all events (value, list of possible cuts) by decreasing value, and find the cut which is contained in the maximum prefix of such events by merging the lists.

    You still end up with O(nlog(n))O(nlog(n)) though since you need the sort.

    → Reply

For A problem the first approach of (n-1)//2 wont it fail for many testcases like say 13 which should give optimal answer to be 5 but this code gives 6...someone pls help me out here.

  • »

    MathModel
    5 months ago, show (+2) #
    »

    MathModel
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +6

    The process goes as following :

    (1,1,11)→(1,1,9)→(1,1,7)→(1,1,5)→(1,1,3)→(1,1,1)(1,1,11)→(1,1,9)→(1,1,7)→(1,1,5)→(1,1,3)→(1,1,1)

    So Hao eats 66 pieces not 55 as you claim.

    0

    first, you need to establish a logic that... any number(N) can be represented (splitted into three parts) for base(x), if that N >= 4*x.

    (WHY?).

    Suppose N > 4*x for now. (N == 4*x is very obvious split (x,x,2x))

    imagine M = N / x , L = (N % x) , mathematically L < x ( remainder is always lesser than divisor).

    So, we can represent N = (x) + ((x + L)) + ((M-2) *x).

    Try for any value.

    Once you have established above logic, its just frequency counting question... keeping suffix sum is sufficient to solve...

    → Reply

JeanBombeur
5 months ago, show (+7) #
»
JeanBombeur
5 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+44

I'll write another greedy for F1, which can be seen as a proof for the Bonus solution :

Consider the smallest ii that can be changed, that is ii appears before i−1i−1 and i−2i−2. Then doing the move from ii decreases lexicographically the permutation, but also makes any move from j>ij>i easier (if j>i+2j>i+2 then nothing changes, for j=i+2j=i+2, ii is pushed to the right, and for j=i+1j=i+1 the min of i−1i−1 and ii is pushed to the right).

Hence there is no good reason not to do this move, so do it and start again until there are no moves left. Alternatively, it is equivalent to reducing taking the position of 33 and reducing it as far as you can, then the position of 44 and reducing it as far as you can, etc...)

You can also adapt this solution with a Treap (on the inverse permutation) to pass F2, see my submission

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    askd
    5 months ago, show (+6) #
    »
    »
    askd
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +21

    I don't understand how this is proof at all. I agree that applying the operation on ii doesn't make any other operation j>ij>i invalid, but how does that imply that performing jj first doesn't result in something lexicographically smaller?

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      JeanBombeur
      5 months ago, show (+5) #
      »
      »
      »
      JeanBombeur
      5 months ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      +29

      I'll give three proofs :

      1 : proof by AC submission

      2 : proof left to the reader

      3 : let's denote by (i,j)(i,j) the operation on position ii when p[i]=jp[i]=j, and consider some optimal sequence of operations (ik,jk)(ik,jk). If there exists some (i1,j1)(i1,j1), (i2,j2)(i2,j2) one after the other such that i2≠i1i2≠i1 and j2⩽j1j2⩽j1, we can swap the two operations since actually we must have j2≠j1j2≠j1, j2≠j1−1j2≠j1−1 since j1−2j1−2 was placed before them, so the two operations are disjoint.

      Hence by noting (i,j→j′)(i,j→j′) some sequence (i,j),(i,j−2),…,(i,j′+2)(i,j),(i,j−2),…,(i,j′+2), there exists some optimal sequence of operations of the form (i1,1→v1),(i2,2→v2),…,(in,n→vn)(i1,1→v1),(i2,2→v2),…,(in,n→vn)

      Now for such a sequence, if some (i,j)(i,j) is not reduced as much as it could, reducing it further only makes subsequent reducing easier (this is in essence my discussion above, since every subsequent value must be to the left of all values it skips on the way down), hence reducing each value as much as you can is optimal (any reduction at position i′<ii′<i will still happen, so the prefix stays the same and the value at position ii decreases).

      → Reply

        5 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        Is this proof correct? Lemma 1:- A operation decreases number of inversions in the permutation by 2

        Lemma 2: Applying operation on increasing order of values will never kill a operation that I could have done before of doing this while the inverse is not true.

        Now, As each operation decreases my no. of inversion by 2. if we could maximize the number of operation that we could do we are bound to achieve the lower bound in number of inversions which will give the lexicographically minimal.

        → Reply
        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          JeanBombeur
          5 months ago, show #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          JeanBombeur
          5 months ago, hide # ^ |
          ← Rev. 2 →  
          +1

          Minimizing the number of inversions doesn't necessarily yield the lexicographically minimal permutation, [1,5,4,3,2][1,5,4,3,2] has more inversions than [2,1,3,4,5][2,1,3,4,5] while being smaller.

          And since swapping two operations actually changes the resulting permutation, you still need to show that it is optimal to do them in increasing order, which is what I clarified above .(Also you have to allow applying ii, then i−2i−2, then i−4i−4, etc in order from the same position, so it cannot be all increasing)

          → Reply

Draunger
5 months ago, show (+4) #

Draunger

← Rev. 2 →  
0

Can anyone give me some methods how to approach the problem C?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    sushant4599
    5 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    sushant4599
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 4 →  
    +8

    Suppose we want a number 'X' to be the answer then either all the numbers should be divisible by 'X' or we are able to break down these numbers into multiples of X, then only it can be our answer.

    First observation is that firstly remove all the numbers < X, as if any of them are present then "X" can't be the answer. Hence, if you have 'Y' numbers < 'X', then apply 'K' operations to remove them . If 'K' < 'Y', then no matter what you do you can't remove all numbers < X, so it can never be the answer.

    Second observation is that, after trying out few examples you will see that if you want to break a number 'Y' into multiple 'X' then it should be at least 4*X.

    PROOF :

    For a number 'Y' which is not divisible by 'X', then for it to be converted into a multiple of 'X' you want it to break such that the non-multiple multiple part of 'Y' is in the middle. Hence, one possible greedy construction :
    First part = 'X' (smallest)
    Second part = 'X' + non-divisible part (second largest)
    Third part = multiple of 'X' >= second part (largest part)

    Looking at this gives us that the third part would be bare minimum 2*X, as second part is always greater than 'X' so, the nearest multiple divisible by 'X' and greater than 2nd part, would be 2*X.

    SO, summing these up gives : X + (X + non-divisible part) + 2*X, which is gives us a lower bound of 4*X.

    Hence, we don't care about any number >= 4*X, as, we can always make them divisible by 'X'. So, we are left with numbers smaller < 4*X, as we can't change them, only option left with us is to delete non-divisible ones. Hence, we check that if remaining numbers other than the ones >= 4*X, and also, multiples of 'X' less than 4*X, i.e. freq[X] + freq[2*X] + freq[3*X], are <= 'K' or not. If YES then this 'X' can be our answer else it can't be.

    Here's my code. Hope this helps!

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Draunger
      5 months ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      Draunger
      5 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      bro why it should not be 3X , why it would be 4X always

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        sushant4599
        5 months ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        sushant4599
        5 months ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 2 →  
        0

        Suppose we have a number Y such that 3*X < Y < 4*X i.e. YmodX≠0YmodX≠0.

        Now, if we try to partition it into three parts such that Y=Y1+Y2+Y3Y=Y1+Y2+Y3 and Y1≤Y2≤Y3Y1≤Y2≤Y3, then we must make sure that both Y1Y1 and Y3Y3 are multiples of XX, which obviously leaves the middle part Y2Y2 with the non-divisible remainder (since YY itself isn’t divisible by XX).

        REMEMBER : We want multiples of XX at both the end, hence Y1Y1 and Y3Y3 must be multiples of XX

        So, we first assign the lowest possible multiple of XX at the start: Y1=XY1=X. This leaves us with the remaining part : Y−X∈(2X,3X)Y−X∈(2X,3X).

        Now, let the remainder R=YmodXR=YmodX. We’ll have the non-divisible part (the remainder) somewhere inside (in the middle). But leaving it alone would make it smaller than Y1Y1, since any remainder is always less than the divisor (R<XR<X). Hence, Y2=RY2=R would violate the condition Y2≥Y1Y2≥Y1. So, we must increase Y2Y2 without affecting the multiplicity of Y1Y1 and Y3Y3. The only simple way to do that is to add another XX to it i.e. Y2=X+RY2=X+R.

        Now, the last part becomes:

        Y3=Y−Y1−Y2Y3=Y−Y1−Y2
        Y3=Y−X−(X+R)Y3=Y−X−(X+R)
        Y3=Y−2X−RY3=Y−2X−R

        Since, we assumed Y∈(3X,4X)Y∈(3X,4X), this means now, Y3<4X−2X−R<2X−RY3<4X−2X−R<2X−R.
        But Y2=X+RY2=X+R, and because R>0R>0, we get Y2>XY2>X. But having Y3<Y2Y3<Y2 is a violation, breaking the original order of Y1≤Y2≤Y3Y1≤Y2≤Y3.

        Hence, for any Y<4XY<4X, such a valid partition is IMPOSSIBLE!.

        Now, if Y≥4XY≥4X, we can take :
        Y1=XY1=X, Y2=X+RY2=X+R, and Y3=Y−2X−RY3=Y−2X−R.
        Here Y3≥2XY3≥2X, so the ordering holds (Y1≤Y2≤Y3Y1≤Y2≤Y3), and both Y1Y1 and Y3Y3 are multiples of XX.

        Hence, the minimum threshold for proper division of YY into Y1,Y2Y1,Y2, and Y3Y3 is Y >= 4*X.
        You may try out few examples yourself to see it more clearly!
        Hope this helped :)

        → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      subtomyonlycodes
      4 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      subtomyonlycodes
      4 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      great explanation actually

Till this date, I was wrong about the sum of (n + n / 2 + n / 4 + n / 8 + ... ), which is actually 2n. I thought this series is same as (n + n / 2 + n / 3 + n / 4 + ...) which is nlog(n). So I missed the simple solution of problem D.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    MathModel
    5 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    MathModel
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    You're mixing between :

    ∑i=0∞n2i∑i=0∞n2i

    and

    ∑i=0∞⌊n2i⌋∑i=0∞⌊n2i⌋

    The first can be obtained with G.P sums to end up with 2n2n

    the second is obviously less , since either of the following cases will happen :

    • nn is a power of two , hence the sum will be n+n2+n4+...+1=2n−1n+n2+n4+...+1=2n−1

    • there exists a power of two jj such that nmod2j≠0nmod2j≠0 so the sum will be off by at least two (i.e. <2n−1<2n−1).

    Thus in all cases the sum will be <2n<2n.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Westxrn
      4 months ago, show (+2) #
      »
      »
      »
      Westxrn
      4 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      but in the case where its ceil(n/2^i), the sum can be larger than 2*n. Thus the editorial for D is incorrect. When you plug in the value 34 for the formula in hint 3, you get 69 which is not <= 2*34. So for some values of n, the editorial solution exceeds the query limit.

      → Reply

        4 months ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        MathModel
        4 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        uh , It's floor not ceil though and floor ≤≤ ceil , obviously in our case it's <<

        ∑i=0∞⌊n2i⌋∑i=0∞⌊n2i⌋

        if we took your example ,

        ⌊341⌋+⌊342⌋+⌊344⌋+⌊348⌋+⌊3416⌋+⌊3432⌋⌊341⌋+⌊342⌋+⌊344⌋+⌊348⌋+⌊3416⌋+⌊3432⌋

        so we've

        34+17+8+4+2+1=66<2×34=6834+17+8+4+2+1=66<2×34=68

        so it still holds.

Can anyone help me spot out edge case for my code, it fails at test-5

Code


const int N=2e5+1;
vector<vector<int>>adj(N);
void sieve(){
    for(int i=2;i<N;++i){
        for(int j=i;j<min(N,3*i+1);j+=i){
            adj[j].pb(i);
        }
    }
}

void solve(){
    int n,k; cin>>n>>k;
    vector<int>a(n);
    for(int &i:a) cin>>i;
    sort(all(a));
    map<int,int>mp;
    for(int i:a){
        for(int j:adj[i]){
            mp[j]++;
        }
    }
    int ans=1;
    for(auto& m:mp){
        int idx=lower_bound(all(a),4*m.ff)-a.begin();
        if(m.ss+n-idx>=n-k){
            ans=max(ans,m.ff);
        }
    }
    cout<<ans<<endl;
}
What I did

Linked every number with its factor (only till its 3rd multiple) using sieve, Computed frequency for every factor, For each factor (x) used binary search to count number of elements >= 4*x

→ Reply
»
DSG213
5 months ago, show (+2) #
»
DSG213
5 months ago, hide # |
 
+26

It is possible to solve E in O(n).

We also need to make an observation that the maximum value will be achieved on the 4-th move.

Just like in the editorial let's first consider a game where Alex goes first. Now the game is reduced to one move by Alex, one block by Hao, and final move by Alex. Let's compute the value for each index i that Alex will take on the first move. To do that we need to know 2 smallest values for all j < i and 2 largest values for j > i. This can be easily done in O(n), we will also need to store the indices of largest and smallest values.

Now for each i we compute an array of possible second moves. This will be up to 4 possible moves. We know that Hao will remove the largest element from the list, so the answer is the second largest.

Now we know what will be the answer if Alex started. But we know that Hao is starting and it is optimal for him to disturb Alex'es best move. This can happen if Hao removes best starting move for Alex, or one of 4 possible values that Alex would take on 2nd move.

So we solve the problem again with 5 possible elements removed. Each Alex starting solution takes O(n) time and will be done at most 6 times. 345606017

→ Reply

There's probably a typo in hint 3 of task E. Or maybe I misunderstood something?

This is because if there are any index i with fg(a,i)>2fg(a,i)>2, Hao has to remove index i on his first move.

we need fg(a,i)≥2fg(a,i)≥2 instead of fg(a,i)>2fg(a,i)>2

qimi_MasterD
5 months ago, show #

qimi_MasterD
5 months ago, hide # |
 
0

I have an alternative solution for problem E.

Hint 1

The answer for the problem can be determined within the first 44 moves.

Hint 2

Compute fjfj as the maximum beauty of the array when Hao chooses index jj as his first move. The answer is then min1≤j≤n(fj)min1≤j≤n(fj).

Solution

Read the hints, our goal is to compute fjfj.

Fix an index ii as Alex's first move (in the second turn), and define:

  • l1l1, l2l2, and l3l3 as the indices of the first, second, and third smallest values in the range [1,i−1][1,i−1].
  • r1r1, r2r2, and r3r3 as the indices of the first, second, and third largest values in the range [i+1,n][i+1,n].

The reason for choosing a triplet will be explained shortly. Let valjvalj denotes the beauty for index jj, defined as valj=ai−ajvalj=ai−aj for j<ij<i and valj=aj−aivalj=aj−ai for i<ji<j.

In some cases (such as i=1i=1), some indices may not exist. However, without loss of generality, assume that all 66 indices are valid. Now consider:

vall1,vall2,vall3,valr1,valr2,valr3vall1,vall2,vall3,valr1,valr2,valr3

These are the 66 best choices for Alex in his fourth turn. Before that, Hao has 22 turns to eliminate 22 numbers. Since he wants to minimize the beauty, he will eliminate the 22 largest values.

Sort the above values in descending order. Let x1x1, x2x2, and x3x3 be the indices corresponding to the 33 largest values (where each xk∈l1,l2,l3,r1,r2,r3xk∈l1,l2,l3,r1,r2,r3). The reason for choosing a triplet is to guarantee that x1x1, x2x2, and x3x3 exist.

Let us analyze how this affects fjfj by considering jj as Hao's first move.

Case 1: j=ij=i

This is invalid since ii is Alex's first move, so fifi remains unchanged.

Case 2: j=x1j=x1 or j=x2j=x2

In this case, Hao's first move eliminates either x1x1 or x2x2. Therefore, after 22 moves (first and third turns), both ax1ax1 and ax2ax2 are eliminated, leaving the maximum beauty for Alex as valx3valx3.

Thus, we update fx1=max(fx1,valx3)fx1=max(fx1,valx3) and fx2=max(fx2,valx3)fx2=max(fx2,valx3).

Case 3: j≠ij≠i, j≠x1j≠x1, and j≠x2j≠x2

In this case, Hao's first move does not remove either x1x1 or x2x2. Therefore, in his third turn, he will remove x1x1 since it has the maximum value. Consequently, Alex will select ax2ax2 in his fourth turn, giving a maximum beauty of valx2valx2.

Therefore, we update fj=max(fj,valx2)fj=max(fj,valx2) for all jj satisfying this case.

I used a Lazy Segment Tree to handle Case 3. If anyone has a more optimal approach, please tell me.

My implementation: 345835104

→ Reply

    Okay I got this. Also the editorial's 3rd and 4th paragraph could have been made a lot simpler rather than just confusing math. The thing is you filter out the numbers that have different last bit because they can't be the last Pn number because at least one bit is different. We will follow this approach till the highest bit and generate the number.

al95ireyiz

← Rev. 2 →  
0

Actually, I found an O(n) approach for problem E. First, we need to observe that the optimal answer can be achieved in no more than 4 moves. Define F(x, y) = b[max(x, y)] - b[min(x, y)] . Lets just assume there was two indices i and j where F(i, j) is maximized. Then, optimally, Hao would delete either i or j depending on frequencies of values in these indices (we don't care about it). Let's just assume j is deleted and i is remaining. Alex will lock index i. Next, define k as the second best index that makes F(i, k) maximized. Hao would optimally delete this index in his next move. Finally, define q as the third index that makes F(i, q) maximized. Alex will lock this index. Then, because Alex locked i and q, our best answer depends on neither which indices Hao erases nor Alex locks.

As in editorial, lets just say Hao will make the answer to be least of the answers for when Alex starts with arrays that some x is deleted. So, lets just focus on finding that indices which are worth trying. It turns out for some x we need two minimums in range [1, x - 1] and two maximums [x + 1, n], because Hao deletes no more than 2 elements. So, we just can precompute prefix minimums and suffix maximums, find the best index that the second best is maximized, and just try to delete this element, first minimum in range [1, x - 1], second minimum in range [1, x - 1], first maximum in range [x + 1, n] and second maximum in range [x + 1, n].

Total complexity would be O(n + 5n) = O(n). Here is my submission: 345943514

→ Reply
»

SoleProprietor
5 months ago, show #

SoleProprietor
5 months ago, hide # |
 
0

Problem E. For the O(n) solution of E, one just needs to look at 3 possible first moves by P1.

First solve the simple game in which P2 chooses, P1 blocks, P2 chooses. The best strategy for P2 in this simple game is to choose the index that yields the second best payoff as P1 can block the first best.

P1 only cares to disrupt this best move. The three possible moves for P1 are to 1) pick the argmax of the simple game, 2) pick the other index that gives the first best payoff, 3) pick the other index that gives the second best payoff.

These are the only moves that disrupt the strategy of P2 in the simple game. Python submission

→ Reply
»

Can someone explain why this code is working for 2156A - Pizza Time

int n; cin>>n;
int m=n-2;
int c=1;
while(m>2){
    m-=2;
    c++;
}
cout<<c<<endl;

It performs around 10^11 operations in the worst case!!

→ Reply

    Compilers will optimize certain statements, for example...

    #include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
int main()
{
    int sum=0;
    for(int i=1;i<=10000000;i++)
        for(int j=1;j<=1000000000000;j++) 
            sum++;
    cout<<sum<<endl;
    return 0;
}

"pizza time"

→ Reply
»
Karan2527
4 months ago, show #
»
Karan2527
4 months ago, hide # |
 
0

To make sure Hao eats as much as possible, he needs to keep the leftover group (m_3) as small as he can. Whatever is left over becomes the pizza for the next day. By picking the two smallest groups, (m_1) and (m_2), to be as big as possible, we limit (m_3) to at most 1 or 2 slices. This means the total amount of pizza decreases by about 2 slices each day. The game ends when there are only 1 or 2 slices left since no more splitting can happen. Therefore, the number of full cycles where Hao gets to eat is the number of times we can subtract 2 before getting to 1, which is (\lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor). Thus, the maximum slices Hao can eat is ((n — 1) / 2).

→ Reply
»

Regarding F1F1, in solution-1, why is it guaranteed that in next positions we will still be able to change them to their optimal values? Like when we keep updating some position until it reaches its optimal value, during this process, multiple values in the suffix will increment, so why something like this for example is guaranteed to not have a negative impact when we go to the next positions?

Also in the other solution of continuously picking the smallest number, I understand that in an optimal solution if we do 2 consecutive operations on disjoint element, where the first operation is on the larger value, we can swap them to make the small-value operation come first. But for the part of repeatedly reducing some value as long as we can, I understand this does not impact the prefix, but why is it guaranteed to not affect the suffix negatively in reaching its optimal?
